content.body: Geometric constructions use only a compass and straight edge (ruler without measurements). Key constructions include bisecting angles, drawing perpendiculars, and constructing specific angles.

Key constructions:
1. Perpendicular bisector of line AB: arc from A and B intersect above and below AB
2. Angle bisector: arc from vertex cuts arms, then equal arcs intersect
3. Perpendicular from point to line
4. Angles: 60° (equilateral triangle arc), 90° (perpendicular bisector), 30°=half of 60°, 45°=half of 90°

content.title: Constructing 60°
content.body:
Draw line AB. Set compass to AB length.
With centre A, draw arc through B.
With centre B, draw arc with same radius.
Arcs meet at C. Angle BAC = 60°
(Triangle ABC is equilateral)

content.title: Perpendicular Bisector
content.body:
Draw arc from A with radius > half AB.
Draw arc from B with same radius.
Connect intersection points. This line is perpendicular bisector of AB, meeting AB at its midpoint.

questions.question: Perpendicular bisector of AB meets AB at
questions.explanation: Perpendicular bisector passes through midpoint at 90°

questions.question: To construct 60°, start with
questions.explanation: 60° comes from equilateral triangle where all angles are 60°

questions.question: Angle bisector divides angle into
questions.explanation: Bisector cuts angle exactly in half

questions.question: Constructing 30° requires first constructing
questions.explanation: 30° = half of 60°, so bisect the 60° angle

questions.question: Tools used in geometric construction
questions.explanation: Pure constructions use only compass and unmarked straight edge

questions.question: To construct perpendicular from external point P to line L
options: draw from P at random angle
use arcs from P to get two intersection points on L, then bisect
questions.answer: use arcs from P to get two intersection points on L, then bisect
questions.explanation: Standard method: two equidistant points on L from P, then bisect that chord

questions.question: A locus is
options: the set of all points satisfying a given condition
questions.answer: the set of all points satisfying a given condition
questions.explanation: Locus: collection of all points meeting a specific geometric condition

questions.question: To construct 45°
questions.explanation: 45° = half of 90°; first construct 90° then bisect it

questions.question: Constructing 90° at a point on a line uses
options: perpendicular bisector method at that point
questions.answer: perpendicular bisector method at that point
questions.explanation: To get 90° at a specific point, use the perpendicular bisector method centered there

questions.question: Equilateral triangle has angles
questions.explanation: All three angles of an equilateral triangle are 60°

questions.question: What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.question: What is 2x + 3x simplified?
questions.explanation: Collect like terms: 2x + 3x = 5x (both terms have the same variable x).

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.question: What is the result of expanding 4(y - 3)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is 3² equal to?
questions.explanation: 3² = 3 × 3 = 9. The exponent 2 means multiply the base by itself.

questions.question: Factorise: 4x + 8
questions.explanation: The HCF of 4x and 8 is 4. So 4x + 8 = 4(x + 2).

questions.question: What is the y-intercept of y = 3x + 5?
questions.explanation: In y = mx + c, c is the y-intercept. Here c = 5.

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

content.body: A locus (plural: loci) is the set of all points satisfying a given geometric condition. Combining two or more loci defines a region.

Standard loci:
• Fixed distance from point → circle
• Equidistant from two points → perpendicular bisector
• Fixed distance from line → parallel lines
• Equidistant from two lines → angle bisector
• Fixed distance from circle centre → concentric circle

content.title: Locus Problems
content.body:
Locus of points 5cm from point A: circle radius 5, centre A
Locus equidistant from A and B: perpendicular bisector of AB
Locus of points ≤3cm from A: disc (filled circle) radius 3
Locus inside angle and <4cm from vertex: sector of circle

content.title: Region Problems
content.body:
Region closer to A than B: half-plane on A-side of perpendicular bisector of AB
Region within 5cm of line L: strip between two lines parallel to L, each 5cm away
Satisfying two conditions: intersection of both loci

questions.question: Locus of points 4cm from fixed point P
questions.explanation: All points at fixed distance from a point form a circle

questions.question: Locus equidistant from A and B
questions.explanation: Points equidistant from two points lie on the perpendicular bisector

questions.question: Locus equidistant from two intersecting lines
questions.explanation: Points equidistant from two lines lie on their angle bisectors

questions.question: Locus of points 3cm from line L
questions.explanation: One parallel line on each side of L, each at 3cm distance

questions.question: Region satisfying TWO locus conditions is their
questions.explanation: Both conditions must be satisfied, so take the overlap (intersection) of both loci

questions.question: Point inside triangle and equidistant from all sides lies at
questions.explanation: Incentre: intersection of angle bisectors, equidistant from all three sides

questions.question: Point equidistant from all three vertices of a triangle
questions.explanation: Circumcentre: intersection of perpendicular bisectors of sides

questions.question: Locus of a point that moves so angle APB=90°, where A and B fixed
questions.explanation: Angle in semicircle is 90°: locus is circle with AB as diameter

questions.question: Region ≤r from point P and ≤r from point Q (overlapping circles)
questions.explanation: Intersection of two circles forms a lens shape (vesica piscis)

questions.question: Locus of midpoints of chords through fixed point inside circle
questions.explanation: Midpoints of parallel chords trace a smaller circle (or for chords through a fixed point, another circle)

options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form

content.body:
Bearings: measured clockwise from North (000° to 360°)
N=000°, E=090°, S=180°, W=270°

Back bearing = bearing + 180° (or -180°)
If bearing A to B = 065°, bearing B to A = 245°

content.title: Bearing Problems
content.body:
From A, B is on bearing 120° at distance 5km.
From A, C is on bearing 040° at distance 3km.
Draw N at A, measure 120° clockwise from N for B.
Angle BAC = 120°-40° = 80°
Use cosine rule to find BC

content.title: Scale Drawing
content.body:
Scale 1:500,000
1cm = 500,000cm = 5km
Draw bearings accurately, measure and convert

questions.question: North bearing is
questions.explanation: North is 000° (or 360°) — bearings measured clockwise from North

questions.question: East bearing is
Back bearing of 065° is
questions.explanation: Back bearing = 065+180 = 245°

questions.question: Ship sails on bearing 220°. This direction is
questions.explanation: 220° is between S(180°) and W(270°) → SW

questions.question: Point B is NW of A. Bearing from A to B is
questions.explanation: NW = 315° bearing

questions.question: Bearing 150° is in which quadrant direction
questions.explanation: 150° between S(180°) and E(90°) → SE direction

questions.question: Scale 1:200,000. Map distance 3cm = actual
Back bearing formula
questions.explanation: Back bearing is opposite direction: add 180° (subtract if result>360°)

questions.question: Bearing from A to B is 070°. Bearing from B to A is
Point C is due South of A. Bearing A to C is
questions.explanation: Due South = 180° bearing

options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

content.body: Geometric constructions are used in architecture, engineering and design. We use accurate constructions to solve real problems involving angles, distances and shapes.

content.title: Construction of Triangles
content.body:
Given three sides (SSS): use compass to mark arcs
Given two sides and included angle (SAS): draw one side, construct angle, draw second side
Given two angles and a side (ASA): draw side, construct both angles from each end

Circumscribed circle: through all vertices (circumcircle) — use perpendicular bisectors
Inscribed circle: touches all sides (incircle) — use angle bisectors

content.title: Regular Polygons
content.body:
Square: construct 90° then equal sides
Regular hexagon: compass radius = side length, 6 arcs around circle
Regular pentagon: 360°/5=72° internal exterior angle approach

Tiling/tessellation: only triangles, squares, hexagons tile the plane regularly

questions.question: To construct an equilateral triangle on AB, set compass to length AB and draw arcs from
questions.explanation: Equal arcs from A and B intersect at C, where AC=BC=AB — equilateral triangle

questions.question: Circumscribed circle of triangle passes through
questions.explanation: Circumcircle: circle passing through all three vertices, centre = circumcenter

questions.question: Incentre is the intersection of
questions.explanation: Incentre: centre of inscribed circle, found at intersection of angle bisectors

questions.question: Regular hexagon: set compass to circle radius. Number of arcs needed around circumference
questions.explanation: Hexagon side = circle radius → 6 equal steps around circumference

questions.question: Only regular polygon that does NOT tessellate alone
questions.explanation: Pentagons have interior angles of 108°; 360÷108=3.33 (not integer) → cannot tile without gaps

questions.question: Centroid divides each median in ratio
questions.explanation: Centroid is ⅔ of the way along each median from vertex

questions.question: To construct 75°: construct
questions.explanation: 75° = 60°+15° or equivalently 90°-15°. Bisect 30° to get 15°, add to 60°

questions.question: Orthocentre is intersection of
questions.explanation: Orthocentre: intersection of the three altitudes of a triangle

questions.question: For SSS triangle construction, three sides given a,b,c. First draw side a, then
options: draw arc radius b from one end, arc radius c from other
questions.answer: draw arc radius b from one end, arc radius c from other
questions.explanation: Two arcs intersect at third vertex when radii equal other two sides

questions.question: A tessellation covers a plane without gaps or overlaps. This requires angles around each meeting point to sum to
questions.explanation: Angles around a point must sum to exactly 360° for tessellation to work

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Loci Construction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Loci Construction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of mathematics.
Core Theory

This section of Loci Construction builds on foundational knowledge. In mathematics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Loci Construction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Loci Construction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Loci Construction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Loci Construction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.question: What is 2x + 3x simplified?
questions.explanation: Collect like terms: 2x + 3x = 5x (both terms have the same variable x).

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.question: What is the result of expanding 4(y - 3)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is 3² equal to?
questions.explanation: 3² = 3 × 3 = 9. The exponent 2 means multiply the base by itself.

questions.question: Factorise: 4x + 8
questions.explanation: The HCF of 4x and 8 is 4. So 4x + 8 = 4(x + 2).

questions.question: What is the y-intercept of y = 3x + 5?
questions.explanation: In y = mx + c, c is the y-intercept. Here c = 5.

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a rectangle?
questions.explanation: Area of a rectangle = length × width.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a like term with 3x²?
questions.explanation: Like terms must have the same variable AND the same power. 3x has power 1, not 2, so it is not a like term with 3x².

questions.question: What is the gradient/slope of a horizontal line?
questions.explanation: A horizontal line has no rise, so gradient = rise/run = 0/run = 0.

questions.question: If a = 4 and b = 3, find 2a + b.
questions.explanation: Substitute: 2(4) + 3 = 8 + 3 = 11.

questions.question: What does the inequality x > 5 mean?
questions.explanation: The > symbol means 'greater than'. x > 5 means x takes values greater than (but not equal to) 5.

questions.question: Solve: 2(x + 3) = 14
questions.explanation: Expand: 2x + 6 = 14. Subtract 6: 2x = 8. Divide: x = 4.

questions.question: The HCF of 12 and 18 is:
questions.explanation: Factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Factors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Highest common factor = 6.

questions.question: What is the LCM of 4 and 6?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 4: 4,8,12,... Multiples of 6: 6,12,... Lowest common multiple = 12.

questions.question: Which number is prime?
questions.explanation: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. 7 is prime (factors: 1 and 7 only).

questions.question: What is 0.5 as a fraction?
